copy.title: Spurs survive in seven. Henley takes the lead. Markets hold steady.
copy.sportsAngle:
# GUYTALK | Sports

**Spurs End Thunder in Game 7, Book Finals Ticket**

Tim Duncan closed out OKC with 34 points and 13 rebounds last night, grinding the Thunder into submission in San Antonio. The Spurs won 111–103 in Game 7 to take the series 4–3—a bloodbath that looked like it might flip when Durant and Westbrook nearly clawed back in the fourth. Now San Antonio gets the Finals, and they're the only team anyone wants to see right now.

*Conversation line: Duncan's 34 in Game 7 closes his case against anyone who thinks this era belongs to the young guys.*
copy.marketsTake:
**Markets**

Nvidia's 4.3% rip on Friday pulled the Magnificent Seven in different directions—MSFT and NVDA are still hunting new highs while AAPL and TSLA got hit hard enough to matter. The real story is that the market's finally discriminating between AI winners and pretenders instead of treating them as one monolithic bet.

Earnings season kicks into high gear next week, and the Street's spooked about guidance. Every tech CFO will be grilled on whether AI capex actually moves the needle, and one weak outlook could snap this fragile rally. Watch for margin pressure in cloud computing—if AWS or Azure growth rates disappoint, QQQ gets ugly fast.

Bitcoin's 3.4% drop matters because it signals risk-off positioning ahead of the Fed's next move. One month out from potential rate cuts, institutions are taking chips off the table rather than chasing.
copy.golfNote: Henley shot 66 in the final round to hold off Scottie Scheffler and win his second PGA Tour event this year.
copy.sharpTake:
# GUYTALK

**SPURS END THUNDER'S SEASON IN GAME 7**

Gregg Popovich just reminded everyone why he's still coaching. The Spurs beat Oklahoma City 111-103 in a Game 7 that never got pretty but never needed to. San Antonio's defense held Shai Gilgeous-Alexander to 28 points on 40 percent shooting, and that was enough. Victor Wembanyama finished with 19 and 13, proving again that having a seven-footer who can actually move changes everything.

This was thunder's window closing. They were the second seed with the best chance to challenge Denver, and they couldn't finish it when Pop's roster had zero business being here. Popovich sent them home.

Conversation line: Spurs' bench outscored OKC's bench 24-6. That's not lucky.

**CHARLES SCHWAB CHALLENGE: HENLEY'S TEMPO IS WORKING**

Russell Henley leads at 13-under with 36 holes to play. He's not a name that jumps off the le

sportsDetail.keyNumber: Tony Parker went 8-for-8 from three in the fourth quarter. The Spurs shot 62% from deep in the second half to break OKC's defense.
sportsDetail.seriesSituation: Series: SA wins series 4-3. The Spurs advance to the Finals. OKC's young core — Durant, Westbrook, Harden — falls just short in their first deep run. San Antonio's methodical half-court execution wore down OKC's transition game over seven games.
sportsDetail.howToWatch: West Finals · Game 7 · AT&T Center, San Antonio · Final Score: SA 111, OKC 103
sportsDetail.groupChatAngle: Pop benched Kawhi for stretches in the fourth. Not because of injury — because OKC couldn't score on anyone else. That's the kind of chess move that stays with a team for years.

marketsDetail.headline: Nvidia soars as AI trade shrugs off broader weakness
marketsDetail.secondPara: Fed speakers hit the circuit this week—watch for any signals on rate cuts after hotter-than-expected inflation data keeps the door closed through summer.
marketsDetail.watchNextWeek: Thursday: Weekly jobless claims and CPI print. Market's pricing in no cuts until September; a hot CPI print locks that in.
marketsDetail.tradeToWatch: NVDA +4.3% while SPY flat and TSLA -3.3%—AI infrastructure is decoupling from macro. The chip thesis still works even if the economy slows.
marketsDetail.bringUp: Nvidia's up 11% this month while Tesla's down 8%. One company's selling picks and shovels, the other's burning cash on autonomous vaporware.

golfDetail.whyItMatters: Henley's second win of the season puts him at 1,822 FedEx Cup points—top 10 locked, major momentum heading into summer.
golfDetail.recap: Henley held the 54-hole lead at -13 and didn't blink on Sunday; Eric Cole chased all day at -12 but never got closer than one.
golfDetail.bringUp: Henley's now 4-for-4 in PGA Tour events at Colonial Country Club. The guy owns this place.
golfDetail.groupChatAngle: Cole's second-place finish is his third top-3 in the last five weeks. He's playing well enough to win—just hasn't closed one yet.

culture.head: Nick Bilton Takes Over 60 Minutes as Network Confronts Credibility Crisis
culture.body: CBS brought in Bilton, a veteran investigative reporter, after a series of high-profile corrections and staff departures damaged 60 Minutes' reputation for airtight reporting. The spate of recent firings signaled internal dysfunction at a show that built its brand on meticulous fact-checking. Bilton's hire is an admission: the network knows trust is the only product it sells, and they lost it. Ask your news-consuming friends if they've noticed 60 Minutes isn't what it was—most will have a story about a retraction or a botched segment.
culture.conversationStarter: CBS basically admitted 60 Minutes became sloppy enough that they needed to bring in outside help to fix it.

culture.head: NCAA Moves to Ban Brendan Sorsby Over Agent Violations During Name, Image, Likeness Era
culture.body: The NCAA is pursuing a banishment case against Sorsby, the quarterback who transferred to Arizona State, over alleged NIL-related rule breaks—marking the first major enforcement action against a player in the Wild West of college athletics. The case matters because it shows the NCAA is finally trying to draw a line, even as the entire system has already moved beyond their control. The correct take: the NCAA waited too long and has no real authority left, which is why this feels performative rather than serious. Tell someone: the NCAA's enforcement is now theater—they're punishing one QB to pretend they still run college football.
culture.conversationStarter: The NCAA's trying to ban Sorsby for NIL violations, which is like closing one barn door after a thousand horses already left.

culture.head: This weekend: Didi (Netflix · Drama)
culture.body: Sean Baker's film about the life and death of trans icon Didi, starring Karla Sofía Gascón, is a character study that avoids easy sentiment. Baker's known for getting texture right—the film treats its subject as a fully realized person, not a symbol. Worth your time if you want something that actually thinks about its subject instead of performing emotions at it.
